title: The Last Sunday after the Epiphany
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## February 14, 2021 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Last Sunday after the Epiphany (also called Transfiguration Sunday in some traditions), falling in Year B of the Revised Common Lectionary. The primary readings would likely include 2 Kings 2:1-12 (Elijah's ascension), Psalm 50:1-6, 2 Corinthians 4:3-6, and Mark 9:2-9 (the Transfiguration account). This Sunday marks the transition from the Epiphany season into Lent, which would begin with Ash Wednesday just three days later on February 17, 2021, making this a liminal moment in the church calendar.

---

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the early months following the November 2018 General Convention's passage of Resolution A003, which had made significant changes to marriage canon law permitting clergy to refuse to perform same-sex marriages on grounds of theological conviction. The Diocese of Colorado was led by Bishop Robert J. O'Neill (who served 2013-2022), and Saint John's Cathedral had been actively engaged in downtown Denver's social justice initiatives. No major diocesan crisis or election was occurring at this specific moment, though the post-pandemic return to in-person worship was still developing unevenly across parishes.

---

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing the depths of winter with typical February cold and periodic snow, though no major weather emergency affected the region in the days immediately before February 14. The Colorado state legislature was in session (convenes in early January annually), focusing on pandemic recovery and budget issues. Downtown Denver, including the Capitol Hill area where Saint John's is located, was operating under COVID-19 restrictions with limited indoor gathering capacity, meaning the cathedral was likely conducting hybrid or limited-capacity services. The local news cycle was dominated by pandemic response, vaccine distribution efforts (Colorado had begun Phase 1B vaccinations in early February), and economic recovery discussions.

---

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

President Joe Biden had been in office for less than a month, with major focus on COVID-19 vaccine distribution, which had accelerated in early February with the Johnson & Johnson vaccine authorization. The second impeachment trial of former President Donald Trump was underway in the U.S. Senate (February 9-13), concluding with acquittal on February 13, just one day before this sermon. The U.S. was experiencing severe winter weather across multiple regions, with Texas and the South preparing for the devastating February 14-20 winter storm that would cause widespread power failures. The national mood was marked by deep political division, ongoing pandemic fatigue, and uncertainty about the economic recovery.

---

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The COVID-19 pandemic continued to dominate with new variants of concern identified globally; the UK variant (B.1.1.7) was spreading rapidly across North America and Europe. The Myanmar military had conducted a coup d'état on February 1, 2021, deposing the elected government and generating international concern and protests. The Middle East remained tense with ongoing Israeli-Palestinian tensions and U.S. military operations in the region under the new Biden administration.

---

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The dominant zeitgeist was one of cautious hope mixed with exhaustion and anxiety. Americans were one year into the COVID-19 pandemic with vaccination beginning to provide some sense of possible return to normalcy, yet uncertainty about timelines and new variants persisted. The political temperature remained extremely high following the January 6 Capitol insurrection and the Trump impeachment trial. There was significant cultural conversation about racial justice, economic inequality, and institutional accountability. Valentine's Day (February 14) added a note of intimate reflection in the midst of collective trauma. For many faith communities, there was a sense that the church needed to address both the spiritual toll of the pandemic and the deep social divisions evident in the political moment.
